questions.question: In Exodus 13, every firstborn belongs to God.

candidates.text: In the ancient world the firstborn son was the heir who carried the father's name; on Passover night the destroyer passed over Israel's firstborn, so God now claims that place by right of redemption. Every firstborn becomes a living receipt of the ransom: where the lamb's blood once stood, a whole life is owed back. The One God spared, God owns.
candidates.source: St. Cyril of Alexandria, Glaphyra on Exodus

candidates.text: Think of the firstborn as stamped with a Passover tag: "Mine." God spared Israel's firstborn, so the firstborn is consecrated back to Him.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 13 says the firstborn belongs to God and ties that consecration to the Lord sparing Israel's firstborn in Egypt.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Sourced teaching lacks an auditable URL and is too long.

questions.question: How many days did the Feast of Unleavened Bread last?

candidates.text: Seven days, the span of a creation week — a whole cycle of time must pass before the soul is wholly purged of Egypt's leaven, the old life puffing itself up. As the Apostle says, keep the feast not with the old leaven of malice but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.
One whole week, no yeast: seven days for the seven-day feast.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=too_long; The idea is fine, but it is longer than the answer needs.

questions.question: God led Israel by the shortest route to Canaan.

candidates.text: The Philistine coast road was the fast lane to Canaan, but it ran straight through fortified war-country. A nation of just-freed slaves would see armies and bolt back to the flesh-pots; so the Man of War leads them the long way round, training them in the desert before He ever lets them fight.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War (Ancient Faith, 2021)

candidates.text: Shortest road, wrong road. God skipped Philistine country because war might send Israel running back to Egypt.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 13 says God did not lead Israel by the Philistine route because seeing war might make them return to Egypt.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Good hook, but the teaching claim is long and not auditable as given.

questions.question: By what did the Lord lead Israel during the day?

candidates.text: Cloud for cover by DAY, fire for light by NIGHT — shade when the sun blazes, flame when it is dark. The same Presence meets you in whatever the hour lacks: darkness needs no shade, so the pillar burns.
DAY = cloud canopy. When the sun is up, think shade.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=too_long; Memorable core idea, but it can be much tighter.

questions.question: By what did the Lord lead Israel during the night?

candidates.text: By night the pillar is fire — the same Presence that burned in the Bush and would settle on Sinai, the Glory of the Lord traveling at the head of His host. Remember it by opposites: when the world is dark, the column burns, for the Light shines precisely where there is no other light.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War (Ancient Faith, 2021)

candidates.text: NIGHT = fire torch. No sunlight, so God lights the road Himself.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The aid is vivid, but the sourced teaching is longer than needed and lacks a usable source link.

questions.question: What did Moses bring with him out of Egypt, fulfilling an ancient promise?

candidates.text: On the most chaotic night in Israel's history, picture Moses hauling a centuries-old mummy-coffin out of Egypt. Joseph had made them swear it four hundred years before — 'carry up my bones' — and now the man who once went down in chains leads the march to freedom, riding toward the inheritance he never stopped believing in.
Picture Moses leaving Egypt with a coffin. Not treasure—Joseph's bones.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=too_long; Strong image, but it takes too long to get to the answer.

questions.question: Whose promise was fulfilled when Moses carried bones out of Egypt?
questions.answer: Joseph's, that he would reach the promised land
options: Joseph's, that he would reach the promised land
Abraham's, that he would be buried in Canaan
Jacob's, that he would see Egypt again
Levi's, that his tribe would serve the altar

candidates.text: Joseph's bones, Joseph's oath — not Abraham, not Jacob, not Levi. The man who saved Egypt from famine bet his very remains on the promise; bind the bones to the name and the name to the promise: Joseph's remains, Joseph's vow, Joseph going home to Canaan.
Joseph packed for Canaan centuries early. He made Israel swear to carry up his bones when God brought them out.
candidates.sourceClaim: Genesis 50:25 says Joseph made the sons of Israel swear to carry up his bones when God visited them.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=too_long; The current line is memorable but still bulky for a missed-question aid.

questions.question: Where did Israel camp before the sea?
questions.answer: Opposite Baal Zephon, between Migdol and the sea
options: Opposite Baal Zephon, between Migdol and the sea
At Elim among the springs and palms
At Marah by the bitter waters

candidates.text: Notice the place they're pinned against: Baal Zephon, 'lord of the north,' a shrine to the Canaanite storm-god who supposedly ruled the sea. Migdol behind, the deep ahead, a rival sea-deity's temple looking on — YHWH deliberately corners Israel at the idol's doorstep, then splits that very sea to show whose hand commands the waters.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Religion of the Apostles (Ancient Faith, 2021)

candidates.text: See the trap: sea in front, Migdol beside them, Baal Zephon across from them.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The historical/religious hook is not auditable from the current fields.

questions.question: Israel camped opposite Baal Zephon, between the sea and what fortified place?

candidates.text: MIGDOL means 'tower' — an Egyptian frontier fortress. Fix the trap by its watchtower: a tower on one side, the deep on the other, a sea-god's shrine ahead. The fortress that pens them in marks the very spot where the sea becomes a road.
MIGdol sounds like BIG wall. The fortified place is Migdol.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; It uses etymology and history without source support.

questions.question: Israel was confident when Pharaoh approached at the sea.

candidates.text: Two million people pinned against the water, dust rising from Pharaoh's chariots behind — and they cry out in terror, not confidence, accusing Moses of dragging them out to die. It is the first murmuring, the same fear that will keep that whole generation out of the land: a freed slave still thinks like a slave.
Pharaoh appears, dust flies, and courage disappears. Confident? Nope.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=too_long; The image works, but it is wordy for a true-or-false recall.

questions.question: Complete the verse: 'The Lord will fight for you, and you shall hold your ____.'

candidates.text: 'Hold your peace' is a soldier's command to a terrified levy: stand still, the Captain fights. This is holy war where Israel does not lift a sword — the Lord is a Man of War, and the only thing the army must do is stop screaming and watch Him divide the waters. Stillness here is not idleness but faith.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War (Ancient Faith, 2021)

candidates.text: When the Lord fights, mouths close: hold your peace.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The teaching angle is fine, but it is long and unsupported as entered.

questions.question: According to the OSB, by what kind of wind was the sea divided?

candidates.text: Mind the Septuagint: a strong SOUTH wind drove back the sea, where Hebrew-based Bibles read 'east.' That wind is the Spirit-breath of God brooding over the waters as at creation, and Paul reads the whole crossing as our Baptism — our fathers were baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea, delivered through the font from slavery into life.
Big S for SOUTH splits the sea. Picture an S-shaped gust shoving the water apart.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The source listed does not support the south-wind note, and the note may distract from the answer.

questions.question: The Egyptians crossed the sea safely behind Israel.

candidates.text: Not one Egyptian crossed: the same water that is a wall for Israel is a grave for Egypt. So the font itself is twofold — life to the one passing through, death to the sin and the Pharaoh-devil that pursue. What saves the new man drowns the old.
candidates.source: Theodoret of Cyrus, Questions on Exodus

candidates.text: Same sea, opposite ending: Israel walks through, Egypt does not.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The contrast is good, but the teaching claim is not auditable in the row.

questions.question: What happened to Pharaoh's army at the Red Sea?
questions.answer: The chariots and horsemen perished in the returning waters
options: The chariots and horsemen perished in the returning waters
They turned back to Egypt unharmed
They crossed and pursued Israel into the wilderness
They were struck blind and wandered off

candidates.text: Egypt's chariot corps was the tank division of the Bronze Age, the height of imperial war-tech. Picture the wheels miring in the sea-floor mud as the walls of water collapse — horse and rider, chariot and charioteer swept under in an instant, the strongest army on earth unmade without Israel landing a single blow.
See chariots and horsemen vanish when the water walls slam shut.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=too_long; It takes too much setup before the key answer lands.

questions.question: Complete the opening of the Song of Moses: 'Let us sing to the Lord, for he is very greatly glorified: horse and rider he has thrown into the ____.'

candidates.text: 'Horse and rider He has thrown into the SEA' — a war-hymn, the oldest victory song in Scripture, sung over a drowned army. The Church makes it the First Ode of every canon: we sing Moses' battle-song because the Red Sea is our Baptism and Pharaoh is the enemy we just watched drown. The soul brought through the water does not argue; it sings.
Horse and rider? Into the SEA. Hear the splash on the last word.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The current teaching adds unsupported historical claims beyond the answer.

questions.question: Complete the verse: 'He was to me a helper and protector for salvation: this is my God, and I will ____ him.'

candidates.text: Helper, Protector, Salvation — God's three acts — then the soul's one answer: 'I will GLORIFY Him.' Theology before doxology: He does all the saving, we do the glorifying. Let the cascade carry you to the verb.
He saved me, so I glorify Him. Salvation leads to glorify.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=too_long; The answer is simple, so the aid should be sharper.

questions.question: Complete the verse: 'The Lord bringing wars to nought, the Lord is his ____.'

candidates.text: 'The Lord bringing wars to nought, the Lord is His NAME.' What He does (ends wars) is who He is. To know the Name is to know the One who ends battles by winning them — tie the deed to the title and 'name' is the answer.
Picture a divine name tag: “The LORD.” The blank is name.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=too_long; The current wording works, but a shorter cue is stickier.

questions.question: What title is given to Miriam in the Song of Moses?

candidates.text: Miriam is called 'prophetess' — the first woman in Scripture to bear the title, taking the timbrel not as queen or priestess but because the Spirit gives her the song. She stands in a line of singing prophetesses, Deborah and Hannah, that runs to the Theotokos, whose Magnificat answers Miriam's timbrel across the Red Sea of history.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

candidates.text: Miriam is not just musical; she is Miriam the Prophetess.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The extra teaching claim needs sourcing and overreaches the answer.

questions.question: Miriam led the women with dance and what instrument?

candidates.text: Picture Miriam at the head of a line of women, timbrels ringing and feet dancing on the dry far shore while Egypt floats face-down behind them. The drum and the dance are liturgical, not casual — this is the first thanksgiving service of the redeemed, music born the instant the danger drowned.
Dance line, drums in hand: Miriam leads with timbrels.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=too_long; Good scene, but it can hit the instrument faster.

questions.question: The people found fresh, sweet water when they arrived at Marah.

candidates.text: MARAH is the very word 'bitter' — the place is named for what the tongue tasted. The name is the answer: no sweet water here, only bitterness waiting for the wood.
They reach Marah, take a sip, and make a bitter face. Sweet water? False.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; It leans on an unsourced word-study instead of a simple recall hook.

questions.question: What did Moses cast into the bitter waters of Marah to make them sweet?

candidates.text: The wood cast into the bitter water is the Cross cast into the bitterness of our mortal life: what was undrinkable becomes sweet the moment the tree is plunged in. Death's bitterness is not removed but transfigured by the wood, as wood lifted up heals the world.
candidates.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

candidates.text: Marah's wood is a classic Cross-hook: wood hits bitter water, and bitterness turns sweet. Remember the answer by the object Moses throws in—a tree.
candidates.sourceClaim: Ambrose says Moses cast wood into Marah's bitter water, it became sweet, and he interprets this through the Cross of the Lord.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Classic typology, but it needs a real source link.

questions.question: Complete the verse: 'I am the Lord thy God that ____ thee.'

candidates.text: The Lord who heals reveals Himself at the very spot where He heals — Marah, the bitter, made sweet by wood. Read the title through the type: the Physician's medicine is the Cross plunged into our embittered nature, and only there does He name Himself 'the Lord that heals thee.'
candidates.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

candidates.text: God posts His name at the healing place: “the Lord who heals you.” Marah becomes God’s clinic sign.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 15:26 says, “I am the Lord who heals you.”
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The thought is solid, but the sourced teaching is not auditable as entered.

questions.question: How many springs of water did Israel find at the oasis of Elim?

candidates.text: Twelve springs for twelve tribes that will become twelve Apostles — fountains enough that each may drink from its own. After the bitter wood of Marah, the soul is led to twelve springs and seventy palms: the Church prefigured as an oasis, water that no longer needs healing.
candidates.source: St. Cyril of Alexandria, Glaphyra on Exodus

candidates.text: Elim is oasis math: 12 springs bubbling in the desert.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The typology needs sourcing, and the answer only needs a quick number hook.

questions.question: How many palm trees were at the oasis of Elim?

candidates.text: Seventy palms over twelve springs — seventy like the disciples Christ appointed and sent ahead two by two, twelve like the Apostles. Fix SEVENTY to the trees that give shade and rest after Marah's bitterness, and the whole apostolic Church appears at the oasis.
Seventy palms = a whole grove of 7-0 trees waving over the camp.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; It uses an unsourced typology claim instead of helping the number stick.

questions.question: At Elim Israel found twelve springs of water and how many palm trees?

candidates.text: Picture a green island in the waste: twelve springs gushing and seventy palms swaying for shade, a resting place after the bitterness of Marah. The springs you drink (twelve) and the palms that shelter you (seventy) — fix seventy to the trees.
Lock the pair together: 12 springs, 70 palms.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=too_long; Nice picture, but it is still longer than needed for a duplicate-number question.

questions.question: On what day did Israel come to the Wilderness of Sin?
questions.answer: The fifteenth day of the second month
options: The fifteenth day of the second month
The first day of the first month
The fortieth day after the Passover
The seventh day of the third month

candidates.text: The FIFTEENTH of the SECOND month — one month to the day since they left Egypt on the fifteenth of the first. Count by fifteens: the Passover's provisions run dry exactly one moon-cycle into freedom.

questions.question: Israel reached the Wilderness of Sin about one month after leaving Egypt.

candidates.text: Out on the fifteenth, hungry on the fifteenth a month later — one month to the day. The number that frees them is the number that finds them empty; count by fifteens and you cannot miss it.

questions.question: Israel journeyed from Elim into which wilderness, where the manna was given?

candidates.text: From Elim's springs to the Wilderness of SIN — beware the trap in the name: this 'Sin' is tied to SINAI, the holy mountain it lies near, not the word for transgression. The manna falls in the desert named for the mountain.
ELIM to SIN: the oasis is behind them, and the manna wilderness is ahead. Think, “Leave Elim, enter Sin, then breakfast falls from heaven.”
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Claims the name is tied to Sinai without support; simpler memory hook is safer.

questions.question: What did the people say they had sat beside in Egypt when they complained of hunger?

candidates.text: They sigh for the 'flesh-pots' — the very cauldrons of their slavery — saying they'd rather have died full as slaves than walk free and hungry. This is Egypt's deepest chain: not the whip but the memory of its meat, the belly that begs to go back. The soul half-freed still craves the savory pots of its old bondage.
candidates.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

candidates.text: Picture Israel daydreaming about a steaming stew pot stamped EGYPT. The answer is the flesh pots, not rivers, kilns, or wells.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Patristic-style application is vivid but not source-auditable as written.

questions.question: In chapter 16 Israel went from singing God's praises to complaining about hunger.

candidates.text: Weeks earlier this crowd sang 'horse and rider He has thrown into the sea'; now the same throats grumble over an empty supper. From the Song of the Sea to murmuring over bread is the whole arc of the human heart — the warning Paul hands the Church not to crave again what God delivered us from.
Chapter 15 sings; chapter 16 complains. Tambourines one moment, tummy-grumbling the next.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Cross-reference to Paul is unsourced here; a sharper chapter-to-chapter hook will stick better.

questions.question: When did the manna appear each day?

candidates.text: Bread with the MORNING dew, meat with the EVENING dusk — God runs a two-shift kitchen in the desert. Manna comes with the rising light, quail with the falling: tie bread to dawn.

questions.question: When did the quail appear?

candidates.text: Quail at EVENING, manna at morning — flesh by night, bread by day. The dusk brings meat to the camp, so fasten quail to the falling of the sun.

questions.question: What does the word 'manna' mean?

candidates.text: 'Manna' is literally the bewildered question 'What is it?' — Israel names the bread after its own confusion, for the heavenly food outruns every category the mind can name. The question is not fully answered until Christ stands in John 6 and says 'I am the bread of life': the manna stays a question until the Word makes Himself the reply.
Breakfast falls from the sky, and everyone blurts, “What is it?” That question is the answer: manna.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Good core idea, but the John 6 typology needs support and the aid is wordy.

questions.question: Israel could store leftover manna for the next day without it spoiling.

candidates.text: Hoarded manna breeds WORMS and stinks by morning — only the Sabbath portion keeps. You cannot stockpile this bread; gather it fresh each dawn. The whole catechism in a food: 'give us this day our daily bread.'
Secret stash = stink and worms by sunrise. Manna was not pantry food; it had to be gathered fresh.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Ends with an added Scripture allusion without source; tighter image works better.

questions.question: How much manna did Israel gather on the sixth day?
options: The same as every other day

candidates.text: Sixth day, DOUBLE portion — gather twice so the seventh can be wholly rest. The Sabbath is baked into the manna before Sinai ever legislates it: rest is something God lays up in advance.
Day SIX, scoop TWICE. Double on the sixth so the seventh stays Sabbath-quiet.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Adds an unsourced teaching claim; a shorter Sabbath hook is cleaner.

questions.question: According to Exodus 16:8, against whom was Israel's murmuring truly directed?

candidates.text: Picture the people shaking their fists at Moses and Aaron — but Moses turns the fists aside: 'your murmurings are not against us but against the Lord.' To despise the gift is to despise the Giver; the complaint sails past the stewards and strikes the King who sent the bread.
Imagine the grumbling bouncing off Moses and Aaron like arrows, then landing on God. Exodus 16:8 says the complaint was really against the Lord.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_rewrite; Solid idea, but it can be tighter and more visual.

questions.question: According to Exodus 16:8, the Lord gives flesh in the evening and what in the morning?

candidates.text: Flesh in the EVENING, bread in the MORNING — evening meat, morning bread. The verse pairs them precisely so Israel knows both came from the Lord, not from luck or the land.

questions.question: How much manna was each person to gather according to Exodus 16:16?

candidates.text: A homer for each head — and when they measured it, he who gathered much had nothing over, and he who gathered little had no lack. Paul lifts that very line into the law of the Church's charity: equal bread, no hoarding, that there might be equality. Greed gains nothing; need wants nothing.
One person, one measured portion—each tent got its set daily ration. Think “one serving per eater,” not a free-for-all scoop.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=confusing; Obvious internal inconsistency: Exodus 16:16 says omer, not homer; current aid reinforces the apparent typo.

questions.question: According to Exodus 16:31, what was the taste of the manna like?

candidates.text: White like coriander seed, sweet as a wafer made with honey — the desert tasting honey from the sky. The sweetness is a hint of what it signifies: Christ the Bread of Life, the true bread come down from heaven, sweeter than any honeycomb to the soul that eats Him.
Honey-wafer from heaven—basically a desert sky-cookie. If it sounds sweet, pick the wafer with honey.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Typology is plausible, but the Christ connection needs support and the aid is longer than needed.

questions.question: Manna was white and like the seed of what plant in appearance?

candidates.text: Picture the desert floor at dawn dusted white like coriander seed, each tiny pale grain glistening with dew — bread from heaven sown like seed on the ground for the gathering. Small, white, round: the very look of manna.

questions.question: For how many years did Israel eat manna?

candidates.text: FORTY years of wandering, FORTY years of manna — bread from heaven the whole pilgrimage long, never failing till the Land. Forty is the number of testing, and every single day of it God set a table in the wilderness: forty matched to forty.

questions.question: Israel ate manna for forty years until they reached the borders of Canaan.

candidates.text: The manna runs the full forty years, ceasing only at Canaan's border when the land could feed them. Paul calls it 'the same spiritual food,' a type fulfilled in Christ the Bread of Life who sustains the new Israel on her own pilgrimage, the provision lasting exactly as long as the journey home.
Forty years, forty breakfasts. The manna kept coming until Israel hit Canaan’s border.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The typology is unsourced here and the aid is longer than needed.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT something that happened in the Wilderness of Sin?
questions.answer: Water flowed from a struck rock
options: Water flowed from a struck rock
A double portion was gathered on the sixth day

candidates.text: Chapter 16 is bread-and-quail country; the water from the struck rock is the NEXT episode, chapter 17 at Massah and Meribah. Sort it by element — here it falls from the sky, there it flows from the stone — and don't merge the two desert miracles, for Paul says the Rock that followed them was Christ.
Keep the desert miracles in separate boxes: chapter 16 rains bread and quail; chapter 17 spills water from a rock. So the NOT answer is the struck rock.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Chapter contrast is useful, but the Pauline typology needs support and adds extra weight.
